Prāṇāyāma in the Six-Limbed Yoga of Anupamarakṣita: Pema Karpo

# Prāṇāyāma in the Six-Limbed Yoga of Anupamarakṣita: Pema Karpo

[](https://substack.com/@tseringdorji)

![Tsering Dorji's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VB0T!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Faedf658f-bb6c-4511-9227-fcdbd4e7c7bd_768x773.jpeg)

### Notes and Context

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

This post presents an English translation of the section on *prāṇāyāma* (*srog rtsol*) from the Six-Limbed Yoga (*Ṣaḍaṅga Yoga*) according to the tradition of Anupamarakṣita, as presented by the sixteenth-century Tibetan master Pema Karpo of the Drukpa Kagyü tradition. Readers interested in *prāṇāyāma* instructions from other Tibetan Buddhist traditions may check my earlier posts. In the present post, I have omitted two of the four ancillary practices of *prāṇāyāma*.

Instructions on *prāṇāyāma* vary across Tibetan Buddhist traditions. Pema Karpo begins with bodily posture and eye-position, features that are commonly found in other systems. He then presents the actual practice of *prāṇāyāma* in five parts: exhalation, inhalation, gathering, vajra-japa, and ancillary practices. While these categories broadly resemble those found elsewhere, both the details of the practices and the ancillary methods differ from those found in other texts and traditions.

Pema Karpo cites a verse from the *Root Tantra* and explains that the **lotus** refers to the body and the **vajra** to the central channel. This passage also serves as a useful reminder that such terms do not always carry sexual symbolism and must be interpreted according to their context. According to Pema Karpo, the goal of prāṇāyāma is not merely to block the movement of the airs in the side channels, but to direct all the vital airs (*vāyu, rlung*) into the central channel and the indestructible bindu within it. The successful entry of the air into the central channel is presented as the true accomplishment of prāṇāyāma and the basis for the subsequent limb of yoga.

***Links to my earlier posts on prāṇāyāma in the Six-Limbed Yoga (Ṣaḍaṅga Yoga) according to different Tibetan Buddhist traditions:***

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Prāṇāyāma

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Prāṇāyāma, the third limb of Ṣaḍaṅga Yoga, is explained in three parts:

1.  Body posture \[controlling the five senses and the vital air\].

2.  Eyes position.

3.  The actual practice of *prāṇāyāma*.

### I. Bodily Posture

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Press the vajra-cord \[perineum\] with the heel of the right foot, while the heel of the left foot supports the toes of the right foot. As for the position of the arms, either cross them over the chest and clasp the opposite armpits, or place the hands over the knees. Keep the upper body upright, bend the neck slightly forward, and press the chin against the larynx. Place the tongue against the palate, keeping the teeth and lips slightly parted.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### II. Eye-Position

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

As for the gaze, adopt the *Amṛtakuṇḍalī-dṛṣṭi* \[Amrta-whorl-gaze\]. Roll the eyes upward and direct them towards the centre of the eyebrows, as though the two eyes were being brought together into one.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lQZz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fcfa3cf80-76c4-40ea-8836-aefedadfc66a_288x380.webp)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lQZz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fcfa3cf80-76c4-40ea-8836-aefedadfc66a_288x380.webp)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### III. Prāṇāyāma

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

As for the actual practice of *prāṇāyāma*, it is taught in five parts:

1.  Quick exhalation

2.  Inhalation and kumbhaka

3.  Gathering \[air into the central channel\]

4.  vajra breathing

5.  *Prāṇāyāma* ancillary practices

#### 1. Quick exhalation

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

While maintaining the bodily posture and gaze described above, exhale twenty-one times at an appropriately swift pace. Visualise that all karmic airs are being expelled and emptied out. Through this, all the residual \[harmful\] airs within the body's seventy-two thousand channels are cleared away, and all the airs that give rise to conceptual thoughts are expelled and dissolve into space.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 2. Inhalation and kumbhaka

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Slowly draw in the upper air from a distance of about sixteen finger-widths and press it down within. Slightly draw up the lower air from below and unite the two. Hold them together for as long as possible. This is vase breathing \[kumbhaka\].

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 3. Gathering \[air into the central channel\]

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Visualise a single black vajra—the central channel, within the lotus of the body \[here, “lotus” refers to the body and “vajra” to the central channel\], and imagine that the openings of all the subsidiary channels in the body are facing towards the central channel.

Then, slightly turn the tip of the nose \[together with the head\] towards the right knee and draw in the breath. Direct the air straight to the navel region and retain it there for as long as possible. Draw the lower air and the mind there as well. When you are no longer able to hold the airs, release them.

Next, slightly turn the tip of the nose towards the left knee and draw in the breath, directing it straight to the navel region. The rest is the same.

Then, slightly direct the tip of the nose forwards and draw in the breath, directing it straight to the navel region. The rest is the same.

Thereafter, visualise that all the airs and essences \[bindus\] of the body gather within the central channel \[Avadhūtī\]. Finally, by means of a yogic technique accompanied by a grunting sound, draw the airs upward and imagine that they dissolve into an essence \[bindu\] at the forehead. Focus the mind there. Then release the air.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### IV. Vajra breathing

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

There are two types: vajra-japa with and without mental support.

#### **(i) Vajra Breathing with Mental Support**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Focus on a Tummo \[*caṇḍālī*\] within the Avadhūtī \[central channel\] at the centre of the navel nadi-cakra. It is as fine as the tip of a hair and about four finger-widths in height.

When exhaling, visualise the air moving outward, circling clockwise around the central channel, like the syllable *ĀḤ* in motion. When drawing the breath in, visualise the air entering the central channel in a counter-clockwise direction, like the syllable *OṂ* being gathered inward.

When retaining the breath with the upper and lower airs united, focus the mind on the syllable *HŪṂ* as the embodiment of non-dual wisdom.

Continue the vajra-japa \[the cycle of exhalation, inhalation, and retention\] until the colour of the breath becomes visible.

#### **(ii) Vajra Breathing without Mental Support**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The second, vajra-japa without mental support, is to settle the mind in the state of the non-duality of air and mind. To do this, first hold the kumbhaka breath. Then, merging the mind with it, rest the mind evenly in a state free from any mental support or object of focus.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### V. Ancillary practices of prāṇāyāma

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

As for the ancillary practices of *prāṇāyāma*, there are four: 1. For the attainment of vast activities, one strikes the vital points of the maṇḍalas of the four elements. 2. For the attainment of immortality, one tastes the nectar of the small tongue. 3. Dispersing the nectar between the eyebrows to attain khecara. 4. For union with the supreme state, one controls the vajra-waves of the throat.

#### 1. Striking the Vital points of Mandalas of Four Elements

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

\[This is a generation and dissolution practice of the four elements\]

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 2. **Relishing the nectar from the small tongue \[lambikā\] to attain immortality**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

At dawn, mix a small amount of sugar and black pepper with the milk of a red cow. Hold it in the mouth, gently swishing it around and savouring it for a long time. Finally, allow it to descend gradually into the stomach without noticing it going down.

As for the visualisation, visualise a moon disc at the forehead maṇḍala. Upon it are the sixteen vowels, from which nectar drips down. Visualise a white syllable SŪṂ facing downward within the opening of the small tongue, which is connected to the forehead. Through it, the nectar flows down and descends from the small tongue.

Then imagine that the entire body is filled with nectar. Move and rub the small tongue with the tongue. Finally, inhale air through the mouth and, at the same time, swallow together with the air the saliva dripping from the uvula. In this way, fill the stomach.

By this method, old age such as the appearance of grey hair and death are put to shame, and immortality is attained.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 3. **Dispersing the nectar between the eyebrows to attain khecara**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

While drawing the breath in through the mouth, visualise that the airs of the five elements, together with their respective qualities, enter through the mouth in the form of their corresponding colours: the lightness of air, the warmth of fire, the moisture of water, the solidity of earth, and the clarity and luminosity of space.

These elemental airs mingle into a single taste with the nectar of the small tongue that descends from the vowels of the moon disc \[at the forehead\]. Draw up the lower air.

At the centre of the navel nadi-cakra, visualise a Tummo \[*caṇḍālī*\] resembling a lightning rod, eight finger-widths in height. When the union \[of the upper and lower airs\] strikes it, imagine that it bursts into flame with a crackling sound, like sesame oil poured onto a flame.

Finally, settle the mind in emptiness.

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

6
